speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Zalman Finkelstein, August 15, 1996. Who brought the materials for building boats? Or did a whole boat come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't build boats.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't. You got a whole boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We, we, we bought little hand boats, you know, met them at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowboats.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rowboats. We bought it. We bought it. The money, money we get. We got money. They used to leave us-- leave money to buy from Italians, you know, an old boat, a good boat that you can put in 10, 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we used to-- we, we get an-- we got orders. You know, used to come a person, and he used to say, be ready for tomorrow night to go out, 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. Now what they did, the, the, the people, they had a boat, big boat, a big boat to pick up people already prepared. They stood in the middle of the ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got rowed out to the ocean?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to go out with the boats. Now this is not far-- not far from-- what-- not far from Naples. It was in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the British had all the warships there. And at night they had these electric going around it. Because they were afraid for being attacked from partisans or whatever, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we have in between those six people, we had a guy that he played the accordion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we used to put them in the boat, 18, 20, 16, 17, on the floor, packed as many. The, the water was that far from there to coming in, you know, on the boat [INAUDIBLE]. But we filled up as much we, we could. And we used to go about three, three, four times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the accordion would play?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was sitting in the middle and playing accordion, and we were singing. Everybody singing, and the lights from the British-- from the, from the ammunition-- the, the, the warships-- on us. And they're seeing us, they're living there. They're going on the water there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did you manage to get onto boats?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I would say we managed at least, in, in the time that I was there, decide no more I feel I'm going to get a bullet in the head. [LAUGHS] So I said, I feel I should stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many times?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, I would say about over 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what boats went? Do you know-- do you remember the names of the boats?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything hidden from-- I did not say what I'm talking to him. I did not say when I'm talking to you. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Nobody talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those two didn't know. I just know what I have to do. He knew what he has to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you decide to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decide to go when I saw, I felt that it's-- I finished with this. I don't want no more. I don't want to do it. So the last boat, the last the day I knew when the, the big ship is there, a ship that passed it, I know. So they connect me. You go now this time. That's it. Finish. You go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. She was killed during the war, killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did it-- where-- did you get intercepted by the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got, we got intercepted. We, we were not far from Cyprus and over there passing by, and an airplane, an English airplane landed. And everybody was down there. You know, people were sitting with [NON-ENGLISH] and things. We were sitting and watching, a few people, everybody downstairs. And on top was like-- it was a, it was a fishing ship, a nice sized fishing ship. And they built, the Italians built, paid money and built the, the, the sleep for the people to lay down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were most, most if something happened, but the outside they had to make believe it's cargo. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put up boxes, covered up, tied down. And this was, was boxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you detained in Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there a few months, a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and again, he got free-- I got free from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because smuggled up as a, a general came in. He was not an officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: A British officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a British-- he was, he was a Jew. He was a Jew, and asked for me. And he showed papers to go to the headquarters. And they took me out. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they smuggled you into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't go yet to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go. We went back to Italy. They couldn't go to Israel straight in. How are you going to go in over there, the British?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get back to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the same thing. What happened is at that time the ship was, was caught. Our ship was finished. And when I came back, I had another ship. And this was [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was not this one. [NON-ENGLISH] was the, the, the second ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what happened. When we got caught, took you to Cyprus. And I was there-- I don't know-- a few months. I made over there the stones. I was stealing those stones from the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there outside, we were playing with them, with the English soldiers in Cyprus. And what happened is they were walking. But they were walking from one, one barrack to the other. They had those stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How long, how long did it take you to get to Palestine? When did you get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you get in legally when you were allowed to go legally, or did you have to smuggle in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They, they, they caught-- this was the [NON-ENGLISH]. The first one was-- the problem what was, first of all, it was too many people, nowhere to sleep enough. Because they brought from other places--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know. They filled up from certain places, people. And it was a mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which boat brought you to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No boat to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I went is, is to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cyprus, and how did you get from Cyprus to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were freed.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were freed from Cyprus. The people, not just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to do nothing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was playing with the stones. I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get to Palestine? By plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British. The British, their boat--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --their boat took us to Athlit. It was a camp, a British camp that they check you, they [INAUDIBLE] you. Athlit, Athlit, Athlit, I don't know. Athlit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Athlit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Athlit. And over there, they check you. And you were in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were, you were jailed in--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was already Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How long were you in Athlit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- two months, a month. I don't remember. I remember one thing. I knew I'm in Israel. I said to the, to the English over there, I have an uncle in Israel. I want to see my uncle. I have nobody. So they let him know, my uncle, let him rest in peace. He had a bakery in Tel Aviv. This was my mother's brother. And I have an uncle. So they connect. And I-- and he came, this man. And he, he-- I stood by the, by the, by the-- this fence, what was on the [INAUDIBLE], the fence. And he's a religious person, very religious person. He, he has a shul in his name in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- [NON-ENGLISH], I mentioned that high, gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they release you to him or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So what happened, he came to the, to the gate. He and I stood by the gate like this on the wires there. He start crying. I start crying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so he said, Zalman, Zalman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he cannot come in. I said, I go inside. And I'll talk to the officers there. They had the barrack for people going in and out and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to them, listen. It's a man. He's an old man. Let him in. I'll sit here with him. So when I got the first connection, I told him the only thing, what they fill from there, bring me cigarettes. That's all what he bring, cigarettes. So he bought maybe English cigarettes, good cigarettes, the best English, full, full-- it was a-- metal boxes, you know, metal containers, full containers like this with cigarettes. And they let him in. They checked him, even though he doesn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't speak. He couldn't even speak Hebrew. He spoke just Yiddish. And he was there from 1920-something. So anyway, we're sitting. And he said, here's cigarettes. Here's this. And then I said to him, you know, I'm going to give those guys to let you in. So I took two cans. And I said, sir, here is one for you. Here's one for you. I saw smoking, they smoked. So they thanked and were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I talked to him. And that's it. But this was my first-- Contact with family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- where did you go from Athlit? You settled in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight in kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kibbutz, Beit HaShita.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Rehovot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rehovot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left-- I, I was, I was married in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in '47, so I was-- my uncle made me the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel you were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. In, in Palestine, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He made me the wedding. So what happened is that while I was in the kibbutz, I get married. I lived in the kibbutz a little while, a few months, I don't know, a year, nine months, 10 months, 11 months. I don't know-- 12 months. I don't remember. I have it written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even more so, my wife didn't like it. She was more a, a city girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she had a sister in Rehovot, in Israel. So if she don't like it, she'll go out. And I know. I was heartbroken. I said this is a life. This is a life that the person can live. What for your work-- to live--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you move to Rehovot, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we both went. We didn't have nothing. You leave the kibbutz, you leave naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And where did you go from Rehovot?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, from Rehovot, then I start working. I start working, and I went into the government in Israel, and worked for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: As what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was managing a garage, tool and die making, a big, big place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Rehovot or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ashkelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ashkelon.

speaker: interviewee
text: This in Rehovot I was working, any work I got just to get in. But then was the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I was later on when I was going to-- I went to Ashkelon. I start working for the government. And the government built in Ashkelon the first shop, you know, with trucks and everything. Because in Israel, the roads and everything, bridges, everything belonged to government. It's not private like over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything breaks, everything is-- it's the government. So they have in every state, in every town-- not in every town-- major cities and highways, parks belongs to the government. And then they make close to it a development of a shop that can fix the trucks. They can-- it makes the asphalt, everything, a big shop. I was responsible on the garage, a big garage. You had three children all born in Ashkelon, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in, in Rehovot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of them in Rehovot. I mean, not living in Rehovot. We took them to Kaplan, in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were born in Rehovot, but they, they were raised in Ashkelon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ashkelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go ahead a little bit, and ask you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how much did your children know of your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask-- did you tell them? Did they ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want them to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they might have known something?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know maybe very little, but very little they know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you, you came to America-- why did you leave for America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find out that my, my sister lives here [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my brother, the oldest from Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let me go see him. I want to see them. So what happened is my wife said, no. I don't want you to go. If we going-- if you going, I'm going with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, but I don't know if I'm going to stay there. I don't want to sell everything. I have a building. I have a house. I live in a, a nice place. I don't want to sell it. I don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sold it. I closed everything up. We all came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Were your children in America raised in a Jewish environment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not religious, but Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what kind of Jewish environment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, kosher, no tref. Same thing like [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friends, their friends, your friends; are they survivors also?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you dream about all the things that happened to you in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you handle the memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wipe the tears, and don't say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one more question before--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, I know one thing. If it's a-- I don't, I don't know if I believe, can say believe. But I believe you have-- I always like to help. I see a poor person, I give him a dollar.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that that helped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not just this, but I see somebody falls down, I help him. You need something, oh, yeah, I have it. Have it. I don't want it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one other question to ask you. Why are you telling your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to do this, either. My wife, this one, she's not Jewish. But I'll tell you something. She's a gold. She said, listen, if you want me to leave you-- you're going to do it. Let the world see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's why you're telling this story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, I told her a little bit to take off from my chest a little bit, points, certain points, not everything. And she walks away crying in the middle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she's the one that she did. And all the things that gave me a little pinch was my ex-- brother-in-law, my sister's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Your brother-in-law. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother-in-law. So we are good friends still. He's already married with a Russian woman. And a good friend, just yesterday I did work for him. I spent a long time, I think almost six hours. I have a little pond, and, and he brought his granddaughter, a little kid. And she wants fishing. And she went-- he went with her fishing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't catch nothing. No. So she knows a little pond I have there, about 100 feet, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, I'll bring her. Maybe she want-- I know she's going to catch some fish there. So but he, he likes to work a little with wood. He works with wood for a few dollars to make. So he bought pipes, 1-inch pipes. And he needed to cut them. And he wants to make cleats on both sides. And I have all the tools. So I made for him the cleats, and stood for about five, six hours. So you are a person who helps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So my wife says, from him you cannot get nothing. But I never mentioned. And she's upset with me. [POINTING] Why don't you say sometime? And I said, I'm not going to say. If it doesn't come from the inside, it makes no sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to show some of the work you've done with, with the stones and the picture that you brought and also speak to your daughter and your wife a little bit. I do want to-- you, stay a minute. I do want to thank you for sharing [DRINKS] your story, your testimony. I'm going to cut the tape now so that we can show the few things and speak to them as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I finish?

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you, very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not totally, a few more minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father and mother. I don't know exactly how old they are. The only thing I can remember is they're young. And this, I found it-- I didn't find it when I came to America. I found a cousin that her mother was a sister to my mother. And when my parents probably got married, they made a picture, this picture, and they send as a gift to her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is Moishe Chaim. And my mother is Pesl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pesl? And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's is Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- I, I worked on it quite a while. And this is to commemorate my parents. On one side is the date, is the name of my father, the date when he died, and the symbol of what he was doing as a professional was this. On the left side is my mother's name, Finkelstein also, and the date when she died in Auschwitz, and commemorates this. And the left little bottom one is Kaddish of the both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This stone, I was living in Italy for a little while. And this stone-- I went to an amphitheater in Rome, while I was living there. And I found this stone over there, not far from the frame of the building. And I made this to commemorate the, the, the, the, the people-- all those people that died in Auschwitz. And this is the, the oven, the way it looked from looking in the front. And the 6,000 Jewish people candles are lit up.

speaker: interviewer
text: 6 million.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 million.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you make this Mr. Finkelstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cyprus. I had plenty of time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Finkelstein, would you be good enough to introduce your wife and your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near me right here is my wife that I love and cherish her. And this is Maryanne Finkelstein, and I love her forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter that I love her dearly. And this is Pesia Finkelstein, and I wish her very well and everything. So far everything is good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me start with you, Pesia. You grew up in a household where both parents were survivors. How much of your father's story did you know growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's so difficult. But growing up, I really didn't have any kind of understanding of his experiences and the losses that he'd suffer, which in the long run it was also our whole family suffers. I knew growing up that he had a very large family. I never had the opportunity to meet-- and the honor to meet my grandparents. I would have had so many uncles and aunts, and I would have had-- I missed out on having so many cousins and a large family. Although growing up, I really didn't feel the pain of it. But it was always there. And I think the first experience that I had of a little bit of understanding growing up in Israel was when Eichmann was brought into trial. And it was, it was a very big thing growing in Israel and, and experiences, Eichmann being in Israel and the stories. But I still didn't experience, really, his stories. Because he really never shared his, his feeling, I guess, because he felt that we were too young to understand, and, and he wasn't ready to really speak about it. Another experience growing up I had was I bought this striped outfit, a suit. And my mother should rest in peace-- I remember bringing it home. It had stripes on it. And she said to me-- I must have been about 18 years old. She says, please don't wear that. Because this looks like your father's pajamas or whatever they called those outfits that they wore. And she didn't let me wear this outfit. I don't know if you remember that. And now, at this point in my life, only now I'm really starting to feel the pain when, when I look at the number on his hand. And I don't know how these people manage to be normal, to raise a family, to raise children, and, and work so hard for the family and, and have this in their heart and have it in their mind and, and still function.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you and your siblings talk about it ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I find that I talk about it more to my friends. Many times we sit-- to my husband a lot-- we sit there. As a matter of fact, I just purchased the tape I, I just-- and I gave it to my dad. And I made sure I told him to give it-- you gave it to your brother-in-law, to Murray. He has that tape. I'm very touched by it. It's, it's very traumatic. I can't, I can't picture in my mind how they survived it, how they lived through that. I, I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask Mrs. Finkelstein--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maryanne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maryanne Finkelstein, this culture was not part of yours, and yet you live in it right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zal has not said much. When we, when we look at movies together, I always ask him if he, if he wants to look at it. Because it's not necessary for him to relive this. I believe-- because he always says, as we're looking at it, that it-- they don't even depict a tenth of how, of how it actually was. He did not tell me very much about it, but the few things that he has and what I have read, I cannot imagine how people can do this from one person to another. [CRYING] But I, I believe that God watches over the Jewish people, and he will let them survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have requested to read something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please. This is Psalm 124. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Had not the Lord been with us let Israel say, had not the Lord been with us when people rose against us, [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they would have swallowed us alive for their fury blazed against us. [CRYING] The waters would have engulfed us, the torrent overwhelmed us, seething waters would have drowned us. Blessed be the Lord who did not leave us to be torn by their fangs. We escaped with our lives, like the bird from the fowler's snare. The snare was broken. And we escaped. Our help is the name of the Lord, the maker of heaven and earth.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I want to thank you, Mr. Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
